title: SCIM in HashiCorp Vault standardizes provisioning in platforms
summary: As enterprises continue to converge around identity-centric security as the foundation of their platform strategy, the ability to consistently manage identities and access across systems has become critical. SCIM brings a standardized, interoperable approach to identity lifecycle management. This ensures that user and group provisioning into Vault aligns with authoritative identity providers and...

sections.overview: As enterprises continue to converge around identity-centric security as the foundation of their platform strategy, the ability to consistently manage identities and access across systems has become critical. SCIM brings a standardized, interoperable approach to identity lifecycle management. This ensures that user and group provisioning into Vault aligns with authoritative identity providers and governance systems.
sections.technical_breakdown: Technical detail is limited in the syndicated excerpt; open the source for depth.
sections.impact:
What SCIM beta supports The SCIM beta is available for Vault Enterprise (self-managed) and Vault Dedicated (Cloud/SaaS) customers and includes support for: 

 SCIM client configuration through /identity/scim/client 

 User create, read, list, replace, patch, and delete 

 Group create, read, list, replace, patch, and delete 

 Discovery endpoints for Schemas, ResourceTypes, and ServiceProviderConfig 

 Vault returns SCIM responses as application/scim+json, making it easier to integrate with standards-based SCIM clients. Each SCIM client can only view and manage the users and groups it created. Built for secure, scoped provisioning Each Vault SCIM client represents one external provisioning system. External provisioning systems only manage the resources associated with their own SCIM client, which helps reduce risk and keeps provisioning scoped by design.

Classified impact band: medium. Affected layer signal: general production.
sections.resolution: Resolution detail may be partial in the RSS excerpt. Refer to the original write-up for timelines, mitigations, and verification steps.
sections.lessons_learned:
By  preventing over-authorization, teams can reduce the risk ofbreach .

Modern platforms rely on standardized identity provisioning to reduce platform friction, improve efficiency, and minimize operational overhead.
sections.trends: This item contributes to the broader reliability narrative for production engineering teams.

email_summary: As enterprises continue to converge around identity-centric security as the foundation of their platform strategy, the ability to consistently manage identities and access across systems has become critical. SCIM brings a standardized, interoperable approach to identity lifecycle management.

title: Slack AI: The Path to Multi-Cloud
summary: In early 2023, Slack faced a foundational challenge: serving Large Language Models (LLMs) at enterprise scale with the security, reliability, and performance our customers expect. Over three years, we evolved from basic infrastructure to orchestrating a sophisticated multi-cloud architecture. We didn’t just want shiny new models; we needed a system resilient to regional outages and GPU scarcity...

sections.overview: In early 2023, Slack faced a foundational challenge: serving Large Language Models (LLMs) at enterprise scale with the security, reliability, and performance our customers expect. Over three years, we evolved from basic infrastructure to orchestrating a sophisticated multi-cloud architecture. We didn’t just want shiny new models; we needed a system resilient to regional outages and GPU scarcity.
sections.technical_breakdown:
This required our teams to manage the operational lifecycle, including cross-region IAM roles, balanced routing across model endpoints, proactive capacity planning, and auto-scaling logic.

We used feature flags to slowly bleed traffic over, so if anything looked even slightly off, we could yank it back in seconds.

We kept high-volume, latency-sensitive features on dedicated capacity (Provisioned Throughput) to ensure a consistent “snappy” feel.

To maximize uptime for a global user base, we deployed these containers across multiple AWS regions.

Understanding Provisioned Throughput 
 In the Bedrock ecosystem, capacity is measured in Model Units (MUs) .
sections.impact:
We didn’t just want shiny new models; we needed a system resilient to regional outages and GPU scarcity. To maximize uptime for a global user base, we deployed these containers across multiple AWS regions. By utilizing Bedrock s ability to route across different US regions based on real-time availability, we were able to find capacity dynamically while adhering to our regional data boundaries. Regional Capacity Orchestration: Success with OD relies on the cloud provider’s ability to manage demand across their entire customer base in specific regions, rather than having specific hardware units explicitly reserved for Slack.

Classified impact band: critical. Affected layer signal: cloud — aws.
sections.resolution:
Rollout: Implemented gradual traffic shifts via feature flags and instant rollback capabilities, ensuring 100% availability during the switch.

From the customer’s perspective, the experience remained seamless; they continued to receive high-quality results without ever knowing a complex failover had occurred behind the scenes.

First, no matter how many failovers we engineered within a single cloud, we remained susceptible to any potential provider-wide outage.

As Slack AI scaled to millions of users, we realized that true enterprise-grade reliability and a best-of-breed model strategy required looking beyond any single provider.
sections.lessons_learned:
We faced three primary taxes:

 
 Scaling Latency: Initialization times prevented instantaneous scaling.

Because Bedrock serves as the primary launchpad for new LLMs, we were able to deliver model upgrades and quality improvements to users weeks or months earlier than was possible on SageMaker, directly enhancing the user experience across the entire Slack AI suite.

This granular optimization led to immediate performance gains:
 
 ~10% improvement in quality metrics for complex reasoning tasks.

 ~67% reduction in latency for high-velocity, low-token workloads.

If one model or provider slows down, the system instantly reroutes the request to a better-performing alternative, making the underlying complexity completely invisible to the user while maintaining a seamless experience.
sections.root_cause_highlight: Likely root-cause theme: deployment failure. Because Bedrock serves as the primary launchpad for new LLMs, we were able to deliver model upgrades and quality improvements to users weeks or months earlier than was possible on SageMaker, directly enhancing the user experience across the entire Slack AI suite. More importantly, it removed the technical bottleneck we faced in Phase 2: because we were no longer locked into multi-month commitments, we regained the freedom to migrate features to different models. To bridge the gap, we engineered a Spillover Pattern: if a sudden surge pushed us beyond our reserved limits, excess requests automatically “spilled over” to on-demand endpoints, ensuring we never dropped a request due to capacity ceilings.

sections.trends:
Large-scale outages remain the forcing function for resilient architecture, communication, and automation investments.

Observability shifts remain tightly coupled with incident response quality and capacity planning accuracy.

Demand spikes and capacity cliffs continue to drive autoscaling, queueing, and graceful degradation designs.

title: LogAn: Large-scale log analysis with small language models
summary: Where Large Language Models (LLMs) meet logs, things can break down. Language models are remarkably good at understanding text. So the natural instinct when debugging a production outage is to dump the logs into an LLM and ask, "what went wrong?" It doesn't scale. This article explains why.

sections.overview: Where Large Language Models (LLMs) meet logs, things can break down. Language models are remarkably good at understanding text. So the natural instinct when debugging a production outage is to dump the logs into an LLM and ask, "what went wrong?" It doesn't scale.
sections.technical_breakdown:
Signal 

 Meaning 

 Example Log 

 Availability 

 Service unreachable 

 NoRouteToHostException: No route to host 

 Error 

 A fault occurred 

 Got an XPC error: Connection invalid 

 Latency 

 Delays/timeouts 

 Notification time out 60000 

 Saturation 

 Resource limits hit 

 IOException: No space on disk 

 Traffic 

 Volume/demand related 

 673 bytes sent, 384 bytes received 

 Information 

 Routine, non-critical 

 application connected ok 

 A second pass predicts fault categories for non-informational logs, giving engineers immediate diagnostic context.

Fault Category 

 Description 

 Example Log 

 I/O 

 Input/output operations, file system interactions, or data transfer 

 java.io.IOException: No space left on device 

 Authentication 

 User authentication, authorization, or security protocols 

 Failed to authenticate user: invalid credentials 

 Network 

 Network connectivity, routing, or protocol interactions 

 Request to archive-service exceeded configured timeout (3000ms) 

 Application 

 Application-level events, process interactions, or service calls 

 NullPointerException in com.example.service.OrderProcessor 

 Device 

 Hardware device status, driver activity, or peripheral operations 

 USB device disconnected: kernel driver unloaded 

 Internally, we use a fine-tuned encoder model specifically trained for log label classification, delivering higher accuracy on domain-specific log data.

Run LogAn locally as a standalone CLI or as a Podman container. 

 # Container Usage
podman run --rm \
 -v ./logs/:/data/input/:z \
 -v ./output/:/data/output/:z \
 -e LOGAN_INPUT_FILES="/data/input/app.log" \
 ghcr.io/log-analyzer/logan:latest

# CLI Usage
uv pip install git+https://github.com/Log-Analyzer/LogAn
uv run logan analyze -f "examples/Linux_2k.log" -o "tmp/output"
uv run logan view -d "tmp/output" # Start localhost server for log report The analyze command produces two interactive HTML reports.

Consider this snippet:

2026-02-04T14:18:13Z Processing completed for batchId=run-88421 A regex search for error or exception catches the IOException on line 3.

Whether you want to try it on your logs, integrate it into your observability pipeline, or contribute upstream, here's how to get involved.

Drain: Compressing logs into templates The Drain algorithm builds a fixed-depth parse tree to group structurally similar log messages: 

 Tokenizes each log line.

title: stalld’s BPF Backend: Breaking Free from debugfs
summary: For years, stalld has been a critical tool for maintaining stability in real-time and CPU-isolated Linux environments. Its core function is to detect and mitigate task starvation, particularly for kernel threads that get stuck waiting for a chance to run on a CPU core dominated by a high-priority, user-space application. The original mechanism for this detection was straightforward but crude...
timeline_entries: The solution stalld offers is to monitor the runqueues of each CPU. When it detects a task that has been waiting for an excessive amount of time, it intervenes. The intervention is a temporary, powerful priority boost. s

sched_wakeup / sched_wakeup_new : These tracepoints are triggered when a task transitions into a runnable state. This is our primary entry point. When a task wakes up, stalld records its PID and the current timestamp in
void (*destroy)(void);

}; The power of BPF CO-RE is best demonstrated by how we handle changes in core kernel structures like task_struct . For instance, the member pointing to the task's CPU changed its location inside a substructure across k

} stalld_per_cpu_data SEC(".maps"); The user-space stalld process simply needs to read the stalld_cpu_data struct for the CPU it is interested in (although the queue_track backend is event based, the user space part of s

Conclusion The new queue_track backend marks a significant milestone for the stalld project. By leveraging BPF and BPF CO-RE, we have replaced a fragile, inefficient mechanism with a solution that is purpose-built for hi

sections.overview: For years, stalld has been a critical tool for maintaining stability in real-time and CPU-isolated Linux environments. Its core function is to detect and mitigate task starvation, particularly for kernel threads that get stuck waiting for a chance to run on a CPU core dominated by a high-priority, user-space application. The original mechanism for this detection was straightforward but crude: stalld would periodically read and parse the text output of /sys/kernel/debug/sched/debug .
sections.technical_breakdown:
Its core function is to detect and mitigate task starvation, particularly for kernel threads that get stuck waiting for a chance to run on a CPU core dominated by a high-priority, user-space application.

When a user-space application is spinning at 100% on an isolated core, these kernel tasks can get "stuck" in the CPU's runqueue, waiting indefinitely for the user-space task to yield.

If a kernel thread was enqueued and subsequently dequeued between two stalld polls, its starvation event would be completely missed.

This tracepoint allows for clean-up.
 sched_migrate_task : Tasks can be moved between CPU runqueues.

In high-performance computing (HPC) and network function virtualization (NFV) workloads, particularly those using frameworks like DPDK, it's common practice to dedicate one or more CPU cores to a single, high-priority application.
sections.impact:
Its core function is to detect and mitigate task starvation, particularly for kernel threads that get stuck waiting for a chance to run on a CPU core dominated by a high-priority, user-space application. When a user-space application is spinning at 100% on an isolated core, these kernel tasks can get "stuck" in the CPU's runqueue, waiting indefinitely for the user-space task to yield. Instead of asking the kernel for a giant text dump and parsing it in user space, we can now place small, highly efficient BPF programs at strategic points in the kernel's scheduler code. The stalld user-space daemon simply reads from this structured map.

Classified impact band: medium. Affected layer signal: networking.
sections.resolution: Each new kernel version risked breaking stalld until we manually patched it.
sections.lessons_learned:
Portability with BPF CO-RE : Kernel data structures change between versions — this is the same fundamental challenge we faced with debugfs , but BPF CO-RE provides far better tooling to handle it.

When a task is switched in (begins executing), we know it's no longer waiting, so we remove it from our tracking map.
 sched_process_exit : When a task exits, stalld must remove any associated tracking data from our maps to prevent stale entries.
sections.trends: Large-scale outages remain the forcing function for resilient architecture, communication, and automation investments.

Its core function is to detect and mitigate task starvation, particularly for kernel threads that get stuck waiting for a chance to run on a CPU core dominated by a high-priority, user-space application.
